retrieved_docs.title: Inflammatory Bowel Disease -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The CARD15 gene has been associated with IBD, but because of its polymorphic features, it is not possible to determine which part of the GI tract will be affected. The role of genes in ulcerative colitis is not as strong as in Crohn disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Granulomatous Disease -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) revealed a connection between genes encoding oxidase subunits with autoimmune and auto-inflammatory disorders. Variations of the NCF2 encoding p67phox and NCF4 encoding p40phox have been associated with systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) and Crohn’s disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Corticobasal Degeneration -- Etiology -- Genetic Basis
retrieved_docs.content: Evidence of broader genetic pleiotropy has also emerged, linking CBD to immune-mediated diseases such as celiac disease, suggesting immune dysfunction may play a role in its pathogenesis. [14] Exome sequencing of 2 familial CBD cases identified mutations in Mg² + transporter (MRS2) and zinc-finger and homeodomain protein 2 (ZHX2), both regulated by MIR4277 . [15] These findings underscore the complexity of CBD genetics and emphasize the need for further studies to clarify the roles of these genes in the disease's pathophysiology.

retrieved_docs.title: Ulcerative Colitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The specific cause of inflammatory bowel disease is not known. There seems to be a primary genetic component since the most important independent risk factor is a family history of the disease (8% to 14% of patients). A first-degree relative of a patient with ulcerative colitis has a four times higher risk of developing the disease. Additionally, ulcerative colitis has a higher incidence in Jewish populations than other ethnicities.

retrieved_docs.title: Enteropathic Arthritis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: IL-23 receptor polymorphisms and caspase recruitment domain-containing protein 9 (CARD9) mutations are also associated with inflammatory bowel disease and enteropathic arthritis. CARD9 mutations can activate inflammasomes in response to bacterial and fungal proteins. [10]  IL-23 plays a significant role in activating macrophages. The T helper 17 (Th17) pathway activates innate lymphoid cells, including intestinal luminal cells. These intestinal luminal cells can produce interleukin-17 (IL-17) and interleukin-22 (IL-22). IL-22 protects mucosal cells; however, IL-17 and IL-22 can trigger tissue inflammation. See Table 1. Genes Associated with Chron Disease and Ankylosing Spondlytis for a comprehensive list of genes associated with these conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Ulcer -- Pathophysiology -- Ulcers Related to Dermatologic and Autoimmune Diseases
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic mutations in FUT2 and Mut2 have also been implicated in Crohn disease. FUT2 encodes the fucosyltransferase enzyme, which is responsible for the secretion of ABO antigens. Mutations in this gene reduce the secretion of antigens, leading to altered interactions between bacteria and the mucosal barrier.

retrieved_docs.title: Adenocarcinoma(Archived) -- Etiology -- Adenocarcinoma of the Colon/Rectum
retrieved_docs.content: Environmental and genetic factors increase the risk of colorectal cancer. Many hereditary colorectal cancer syndromes like the familial adenomatous polyposis, Lynch syndrome, and other non-Lynch syndromes like the biallelic MUTYH, BRCA1/2, PALB2, CDKN2A, TP53, FAP increases the risk of colorectal cancer. [8] [9] [10] Other risk factors include personal or family history, inflammatory bowel disease, red and processed meat, tobacco use, and alcohol also increase the risk.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Granulomatous Disease -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: A substantial fraction of patients with CGD experience a form of inflammatory disease that greatly resembles inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), especially Crohn's disease. Symptoms can range from mild to bloody diarrhea and malabsorption. Some patients also describe other chronic inflammatory symptoms, including non-infectious arthritis, gingivitis, chorioretinitis or uveitis, glomerulonephritis, and rarely white matter lesions in the brain. The CGD genotype accentuates the standard genetic risk associated with inflammatory bowel disease (IBD). Granulomata in CGD colitis have sharply defined histiocyte aggregates with surrounding lymphocytic inflammation, unlike the poorly formed granulomata often seen in Crohn's disease. When staining for macrophage marker, CD68, CGD bowel disease had significantly lower levels than typical Crohn’s disease. While patients with CGD granulomatous colitis respond well to tumor necrosis factor-alpha inhibition, such as infliximab, they often have significant infectious complications, sometimes fatal, than typical patients with Crohn's disease. Clinically, it is important to note the relationship between Crohn's disease and CGD since this can affect patient diagnosis, morbidity, and treatment options.

retrieved_docs.title: Cutaneous Crohn Disease -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Although the underlying etiology of Crohn disease remains fundamentally unknown, the largely accepted theory involves exposure to “triggers” (ie, microbial, environmental, immunological) in a genetically susceptible person. The chronic inflammatory component of Crohn disease is likely driven by altered activation of both Th1 and Th17 immune pathways with elevated levels of interleukins 23 and 17. Polymorphisms of the NOD2 / CARD15 genes may further affect the innate immune response. Patients with an altered TRAF3IP2 gene may be predisposed to developing cutaneous involvement of their Crohn disease. [5] Other studies have shown altered intestinal flora (shifts in composition or decreased diversity) in patients with Crohn disease. Precise etiology and pathogenesis are also limited to the cutaneous manifestations of Crohn disease. Still, assistance in understanding and categorizing these skin findings comes from the following three well-accepted categories:

retrieved_docs.title: Extraintestinal Manifestations of Inflammatory Bowel Disease -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: EIMs have a genetic predisposition as individuals with siblings and first-degree relatives with IBD are more likely to manifest these symptoms. [14] IBD and its EIMs are associated with the presence of specific major histocompatibility complex loci. HLA-A2, HLA-DR1, and HLA-DQw5 are associated with EIMs in Crohn disease, whereas HLA-DR103 is associated with EIMs in ulcerative colitis. [15] Various HLA complexes are associated with specific EIMs, such as PSC and AS with HLA-B8/DR3 and HLA-B27, respectively. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Crohn Disease -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic variants are associated with certain Crohn disease phenotypes. For example, NOD2/CARD15 mutations have been detected in patients with Crohn disease presenting with ileal involvement and increased severity at a younger age. These individuals often require surgical intervention. Genotyping is expected to provide prognostic information on disease severity in the future. Currently, genetic testing remains mostly a research tool. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Ileal Atresia -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: In type IIIb JIA, poor outcomes are associated with prematurity, low birth weight, and associated anomalies. [20] Type IV (multiple-segment atresia) is associated with dysfunction of the central nervous symptoms in 25% of nonfamilial cases and severe immunodeficiency linked to a mutation in the tetratricopeptide repeat domain-7A (TTC7A) gene. [42] [43] [44] The familial form of type IV is fatal and is associated with prematurity and reduced intestine length. [45] [46]

However, this depends largely on the gene mutated, the type of mutation, and the position of the mutation within the gene. The NCF1 gene mutations generally lead to milder forms of the disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Familial Adenomatous Polyposis -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic testing for APC gene mutations is essential to confirm the diagnosis in suspected cases and identify at-risk family members.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2874
retrieved_docs.content: The term “vrozené” (meaning congenital) is used as a qualifier for several distinct medical conditions, indicating that each can occur in a congenital form. Specifically, “vrozené” can qualify “قَرْحَة مَعِدِيّة” (gastro‑intestinal ulcer), “تفاعل نقل الدم” (blood‑transfusion reaction), “الْتِهابُ المثانة” (cystitis), and “عضال غدي” (glandular disease). Recognizing these conditions as potentially congenital helps clinicians consider inherited or developmental etiologies during diagnosis, tailor management strategies accordingly, and provide more precise counseling for patients and families.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 4 -- Community 2747
retrieved_docs.content: The qualifier “vrozené” (congenital) is an allowed descriptor for several distinct disorders, indicating that each can have a form present from birth. Specifically, “الْتِهابُ المثانة” (bladder inflammation) may be congenital, as can “تمزق الشبكية” (retinal detachment), “فالج” (paralysis), and “قرحات الطرفين السفليين” (ulcers of the lower limbs). Recognizing that these conditions may arise congenitally is important for early diagnosis, appropriate genetic counseling, and tailored management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2897
retrieved_docs.content: The allowed qualifier “vrozené” (congenital) can be applied to فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الحديد (iron‑deficiency anemia), الخرف من نوع الزهايمر (Alzheimer‑type dementia) and الْتِهابُ المثانة (cystitis), indicating that each of these conditions may have a hereditary or birth‑present form. Recognizing a congenital variant of iron‑deficiency anemia acknowledges genetic causes of impaired iron metabolism; applying the qualifier to Alzheimer‑type dementia reflects early‑onset, genetically driven cases; and using it for cystitis denotes rare congenital anomalies of the urinary tract that predispose to bladder inflammation. This classification underscores that, although these diseases are commonly acquired, there are recognized inherited or congenital presentations that are relevant for diagnosis, coding and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2269
retrieved_docs.content: Aminosalicylic acid and similar agents are classified as members of the sulfasalazine compound, indicating that these 5‑aminosalicylic acid derivatives constitute the active moiety released from sulfasalazine after colonic bacterial metabolism. Sulfasalazine, a combination of sulfapyridine and 5‑aminosalicylic acid, is employed in the treatment of ulcerative colitis and Crohn disease, and its therapeutic effect relies on the release of aminosalicylic acid and related agents within the intestine. Recognizing aminosalicylic acid and its analogues as integral components of sulfasalazine helps clinicians understand the drug’s mechanism of action and potential side‑effect profile.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 4 -- Community 2887
retrieved_docs.content: The qualifier “vrozené” (meaning congenital) is applicable to several distinct medical conditions, indicating that each can manifest from birth. Specifically, “vrozené” can be used to describe a congenital gastric ulcer (قَرْحَة مَعِدِيّة), a congenital form of arterial sclerosis (تَصَلُّبٌ شِرْيَانِيّ), a congenital cystitis (الْتِهابُ المثانة), and a congenital retinal detachment (تمزق الشبكية). Recognizing these congenital variants is important for accurate diagnosis, early intervention, and appropriate management in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2629
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic inflammatory small bowel disease is characterized by a chronic clinical course, indicating a prolonged and persistent disease trajectory. This chronic pattern of disease progression is similarly observed in secondary diabetes mellitus that is complicated by diabetic chronic kidney disease, where the condition also follows a chronic clinical course. The shared chronic nature of these distinct disorders underscores the importance of recognizing that patients with chronic inflammatory small bowel disease may be susceptible to metabolic disturbances such as secondary diabetes mellitus and its renal complications, necessitating vigilant monitoring and integrated management of both gastrointestinal and endocrine‑renal health.
